meaning: The completion of a process or the time taken to complete a task
example: We need to turn around this project by Friday.
example2: The factory can turn around new orders in just twenty-four hours.
example3: They managed to turn around the failing company in six months.
Explanation: In an IT and business context, 'turn around' refers to the process of receiving, processing, and returning something. It can mean improving a bad situation or simply completing a specific cycle of work. It is often used as a phrasal verb to describe the action of finishing a task quickly. Efficiency is usually the main focus when someone discusses the need to turn around a product or service. This term is vital in logistics, software development, and customer service departments.
usageTip: Use it as two words when acting as a verb and one word when used as a noun.
commonMistake: Using 'turn around' only to mean a physical rotation instead of task completion.

meaning: To completely reverse a direction or quickly improve a failing situation into a successful one
example: The manager managed to turn around the struggling business within six months.
example2: Please turn around so I can check the back of the computer hardware monitor casing.
example3: We need to turn around this code error quickly before the presentation starts.
Explanation: The phrasal verb 'turn around' has two major meanings depending on your current environment. Physically, it means rotating your body or an object 180 degrees to face the opposite direction. In business, management, and software project workflows, it means transforming a bad situation, a failing application, or a slow team into a highly productive one. It can also refer to the time it takes to complete a task, often called turnaround time. This makes it an incredibly popular phrase in productivity meetings.
commonContext: Project management cycles, physical mechanics, and system improvements
usageTip: Use this phrase when talking about reversing physical directions or fixing a broken business scenario.
commonMistake: Writing the verb phrase as a single word 'turnaround', which should only be done for the noun form.
